1:
"And he began to speak unto them by parables. A certain man planted a vineyard, and set an hedge about it, and digged a place for the winefat, and built a tower, and let it out to husbandmen, and went into a far country." The witnesses treat the parable's occasion, its allegory, and its aim.

It follows the silencing of the tempters. The Gloss notes that "after the Lord had closed the mouths of His tempters by a wise question, He next shews their wickedness in a parable," spoken, Gill and Lightfoot observe, against "the chief priests and elders," who "knew that these things were spoken of them" (cf. Matthew 21:33). Robertson notes Mark does not mean this was the first parable, only that "his teaching on this occasion took the parabolic turn."

The allegory the fathers read as in Matthew. Pseudo-Jerome: "God the Father is called a man by a human conception; the vineyard is the house of Israel; the hedge is the guardianship of Angels; the winefat is the law; the tower is the temple; and the husbandmen the priests." The "servants sent were the prophets, the fruit of the vineyard obedience"; the "far country" means not a change of place but that God "seemed to go away... that He might leave the husbandmen to act on their own freewill" (Bede). Lightfoot supplies the rabbinic colour — the Jews "boasted that they were the Lord's vineyard," and even fixed the measures of the hedge, the winefat, and the tower.

The aim Henry's tradition states plainly: "Christ had formerly in parables showed how he designed to set up the gospel church; now he begins in parables to show how he would lay aside the Jewish church." Members of the church "are God's tenants, and they have both a good Landlord and a good bargain, and may live well upon it, if it be not their own fault." The vineyard so richly furnished — hedged, provided with winefat and tower — leaves the husbandmen, the witnesses observe, without excuse for the barrenness and violence that follow. Robertson notes the "pit for the winepress" (hypolēnion) is the trough beneath the press that caught the juice, a detail Mark alone preserves; every provision for fruitfulness had been made, so that the fault lay wholly in the tenants.
2:
"And at the season he sent to the husbandmen a servant, that he might receive from the husbandmen of the fruit of the vineyard." The witnesses identify the servant and the rent expected.

The servants, all agree, are the prophets. Gill insists that "by the servant" are not "intended... a single person, but a set of men," for Matthew "expresses it in the plural number, servants." Ellicott notes Mark's characteristic vividness: he "speaks of one servant having been sent, and then another, and another, and then many others, while St. Matthew divides them simply into two great groups" — Mark "seizing on the most vivid presentation of the facts."

"At the season" the commentators explain from the law of the vineyard. It was, Gill and Lightfoot note, "the fourth year from the planting," when the fruit, having been "uncircumcised" and uneatable for three years, became "holy to the Lord" and might be enjoyed (cf. Leviticus 19:23–25). Lightfoot describes the rabbinic practice of marking such a vineyard "by some turf of earth" so men might know its fruit was holy. Robertson notes the purpose clause — the servant was sent "that he might receive" the fruit — and that the verb for "wounded in the head" (v. 4) is a rare one meaning to "hit on the head."

Henry's tradition draws the lesson of God's gentle dealing. "He was not hasty in his demands, nor high; for he did not send for the rent till they could make it, at the season"; "nor did he put them to the trouble of making money of it, but was willing to take it in specie." The rent God seeks — the fruit of obedience (cf. Isaiah 5:2) — is no more than was reasonable from those who held so favoured a vineyard. The sending of the servant is thus an act of patient justice: the Owner asks only what is his due, and asks it at the fitting time, through messengers sent to remind the tenants of their obligation. Henry's tradition adds that these servants "were the prophets of the Old Testament, who were sent... to reprove and instruct" the people, and to "help them in gathering in the fruit, and making return of it" — the Owner seeking not gain for himself but the obedience due from his own.
3:
"And they caught him, and beat him, and sent him away empty." The witnesses read the violence as the long history of Israel's treatment of the prophets.

Gill marks the rudeness of it. The clause "they caught him," though omitted in some versions, he holds should be retained, for it "denotes the rudeness and violence with which the prophets of the Lord were used by the Jewish nation." They seized the messenger, beat him, and sent him back "empty" — without the fruit he came to receive, and bearing instead the marks of their contempt.

The fathers had named the first of these servants. Bede (on v. 1) took him as Moses, "but they beat him, and sent him away empty, because they angered Moses in the tents"; Theophylact, as "the prophets who lived about the time of Elias, for Zedekiah the false prophet beat Micaiah." Whether of Moses or of the earliest prophets, the pattern is the same: the servant sent in the Owner's name is met not with the rent but with blows.

Henry's tradition gathers the sorrowful theme. "It is sad to think what base usage God's faithful ministers have met with, in all ages, from those that have enjoyed the privileges of the church and have not brought forth fruit answerable. The Old-Testament prophets were persecuted even by those that went under the name of the Old-Testament church." Here the husbandmen "beat them, and sent them empty away; that was bad." The verse, brief as it is, the witnesses observe, opens the dark record that the parable unfolds — a record of mercy sent and mercy abused, the Owner's patience answered by the tenants' growing cruelty, beginning with insult and ending, as the next verses show, in murder. The empty-handed return of the beaten servant is the first measure of a guilt that will rise until it crucifies the Son. Such, Henry's tradition observes, "has all along been the lot of God's faithful messengers": to suffer, and to suffer from "their Master's own tenants," the very men who professed his name and held his vineyard, yet rendered him neither fruit nor reverence.
4:
"And again he sent unto them another servant; and at him they cast stones, and wounded him in the head, and sent him away shamefully handled." The witnesses dwell on the second servant, the worsening abuse, and a notable textual crux.

The "another servant" the fathers read as a further company of prophets. Gill takes them as "Isaiah, Zechariah, and others"; Bede (on v. 1), as "David and the other Psalmists," "wounded in the head and shamefully handled" because the people "despised the songs of the Psalmists, and rejected David himself, saying, What portion have we in David?" The abuse has worsened: not merely beaten now, but stoned and "shamefully handled."

The phrase "wounded in the head" the grammatical commentators find difficult. The Greek (ekephaliōsan), Clarke notes, "is found in no Greek writer" in the sense of wounding the head; the cognate verb means "to sum up, to comprise" (as in Romans 13:9), so that many learned men render it "they made short work of it" or "they speedily sent him away." Clarke himself prefers "they made short work of it," while the Vulgate gives "wounded him in the head." Ellicott adds that the words "at him they cast stones" are "wanting in the best MSS., and probably originated in a marginal note explaining how the labourers wounded the second servant"; Lightfoot distinguishes this casual pelting with many stones from the formal stoning of the Sanhedrin.

Whatever the precise rendering, the witnesses agree on the sense: the second embassy fared worse than the first. The Owner's patience, far from softening the tenants, only emboldened their insolence; each fresh messenger was met with a deeper outrage. Bede's reading of the rejected Psalmists draws the point into the worship of Israel itself — the very songs given to lead the people to God were despised, and those who sang them shamefully used. The escalation from beating to stoning, the commentators observe, traces the downward course of a hardened heart, which abuses greater mercy with greater contempt, until nothing remains but to kill. Gill notes this second servant denotes "another set" of good men sent to "instruct, advise, and counsel them, and exhort them to their duty"; the more earnestly God pressed his claim, the more violently the tenants resisted it.
5:
"And again he sent another; and him they killed, and many others; beating some, and killing some." The witnesses mark the climax of the abuse before the Son and the long roll of murdered prophets.

The third servant and the "many others" the fathers read as the whole band of the prophets. Bede (on v. 1) takes them as "the band of the prophets," asking, "but which of the prophets did they not persecute?" Theophylact names "the prophets who flourished about the time of Daniel and Ezekiel"; Gill suggests also those "used in a very inhuman manner... in the times of the Maccabees." Now the violence reaches its height: not beaten only, nor stoned, but slain — "him they killed."

The grammatical commentators note the construction and the witness of Scripture. Robertson observes the "distributive use of the demonstrative" — "beating some and killing some" — and that the verb means "to kill off." Barnes gathers the testimony of the murdered prophets (cf. Matthew 23:37; Nehemiah 9:30; Jeremiah 7:25) — that God "sent his servants the prophets, rising up early and sending them," and they were slain.

Henry's tradition draws the dreadful progression to its point: "they beat them, and sent them empty away; that was bad: they wounded them... that was worse: nay, at length, they came to such a pitch of wickedness that they killed them." The three sendings, the witnesses observe, trace the steady hardening of the husbandmen, whose treatment of each messenger surpassed the last in cruelty. And the patience of the Owner is as marvellous as their wickedness: still, after beating and stoning and murder, "he sent... many others." The verse thus prepares the way for the supreme proof both of God's long-suffering and of Israel's guilt — the sending, after all the slain prophets, of the beloved Son himself. Barnes points to the prophetic summary that "God sent his servants the prophets, rising up early and sending them" (Jer 7:25), only to have them slain — so that the parable is no exaggeration but the plain history of Israel's dealings with the messengers of God.
6:
"Having yet therefore one son, his wellbeloved, he sent him also last unto them, saying, They will reverence my son." The witnesses dwell on the sending of the Son, the climax of grace, and the "they will reverence."

The Son is the only-begotten and beloved. Gill expounds: "the Messiah, the Lord Jesus Christ, who is the one, and only Son of God... his only begotten Son," "the Son of his love, who was loved by him before the foundation of the world." Robertson notes "a beloved son" echoes "the language of the Father to him at his baptism" (Mark 1:11); Ellicott observes "his wellbeloved" is "added by St. Mark to St. Matthew's briefer form," and Robertson that "last" is "only in Mark." This is the householder's final resort.

The sending is the summit of the Owner's grace. Henry's tradition marks it: "God did at length send them his Son, his well-beloved; it was therefore so much the greater kindness in him to send him, as in Jacob to send Joseph to visit his brethren" (cf. Genesis 37:14). "It might be expected that he whom their Master loved, they also should respect and love."

The words "they will reverence my son" the fathers guard from implying ignorance in God. Theophylact: "he said this not as though he were ignorant of what was to happen, but to shew what it was right and fitting that they should do." Pseudo-Jerome reads it as spoken "in irony"; Bede, that "God is said to doubt, that freedom of will may be left to man." The contrast with the slain servants, all agree, only heightens the dignity of this last messenger: where the prophets were servants, this is the Son and heir (cf. Hebrews 1:1–2). The witnesses thus find in the verse both the depth of divine kindness — the Father parting with his beloved for the sake of a rebellious people — and the measure of the coming crime, that men who had murdered the servants would not spare the Son. Gill dwells on the tenderness of the title — the Son "in his bosom, as one brought up with him, and rejoicing before him," whom the Father at the baptism and the transfiguration owned by a voice from heaven as his beloved; that such a one should be sent to such men measures at once the Father's love and the tenants' guilt.
7:
"But those husbandmen said among themselves, This is the heir; come, let us kill him, and the inheritance shall be ours." The witnesses dwell on the rulers' knowing malice and the motive behind the murder.

The plot was hatched in conscious guilt. Clarke notes "they appear to have acknowledged in their consciences that this was the Messiah, the heir of all things." Bede had pressed the point (on v. 1): "the Lord proves most clearly that the chiefs of the Jews did not crucify the Son of God through ignorance, but through envy; for they understood that this was He to whom it was said, I will give thee the heathen for thine inheritance" (Psalm 2:8). Robertson notes Mark's distinctive phrase "among themselves" (reflexive), the secret counsel of the conspirators (cf. Matthew 21:38).

The motive was the keeping of their power. "The inheritance shall be ours," Clarke paraphrases — "by slaying him we shall maintain our authority, and keep possession of our revenues." Henry's tradition reads the same: "because Christ, in calling to repentance... made his demands with more authority than the prophets had done, they were the more enraged against him, and determined to put him to death, that they might engross all church power to themselves." Theophylact (on v. 1) had set the deed "without Jerusalem, for the Lord was crucified out of the city."

The witnesses thus find in the verse the dark logic of the rejection. The husbandmen do not kill the heir in ignorance of who he is, but precisely because they know: it is his rightful claim that they cannot bear. They prefer the temporal inheritance of "wealth, and pomp, and powers of this world" (Henry's tradition) to the heavenly inheritance that reverence for the Son would have secured. So the very dignity that should have won their homage becomes the reason for his murder — a self-condemning calculation that lays bare, beneath all their professions of zeal for God, a naked grasping after their own dominion. Robertson notes Luke varies the phrase to "with one another," a reciprocal pronoun for the same conspiratorial counsel; the deed was deliberated, not done in a sudden heat, which only deepens the guilt of those who took counsel together against the heir.
8:
"And they took him, and killed him, and cast him out of the vineyard." The witnesses read the three acts as the arrest, crucifixion, and rejection of Christ.

Gill expounds the sequence: "they sent their officers and servants, and apprehended him in the garden; they delivered him to the Gentiles, who were without the vineyard, and by whom, at their instigation, he was put to death, even to the death of the cross." The deed was the Jews' own, though carried out by Roman hands.

The order of the words the grammatical commentators note as distinctive. Mark has "killed him, and cast him forth," while "Matthew and Luke reverse the order, cast forth and killed" (Robertson). The two arrangements answer to two true aspects of the one event: the casting out of the vineyard pictures the rejection of Christ by his own people, and his being slain "out of their own borders" (Bede, on v. 1) answers to the historical fact, marked by Barnes, that "Jesus was crucified out of Jerusalem" — "without the gate" (cf. Hebrews 13:12). Theophylact (on v. 1) had said the same: the deed was done "without Jerusalem."

The witnesses thus find the verse the parable's grim centre, the murder of the Son foretold by the very men who would commit it. As they had beaten and stoned and killed the servants, so now, with a deeper guilt, they "took him, and killed him." The casting out of the vineyard, whether read as the order of the acts or as the place of the cross, carries the same weight: the heir is treated as an outcast by those who held his Father's vineyard, thrust beyond their borders as one unworthy of a place among them — the very One in whom, as the next verses declare, the rejected stone would become the head of the corner. The supreme crime of the husbandmen is here stated in the plainest words, and with it the parable turns toward judgment. Barnes refers the casting out to the truth that Christ "suffered without the gate" (Heb 13:12), an offering made beyond the camp; so the very act by which the husbandmen sought to be rid of him fulfilled the pattern of his redeeming sacrifice, turning their crime into the means of the world's salvation.
9:
"What shall therefore the lord of the vineyard do? he will come and destroy the husbandmen, and will give the vineyard unto others." The witnesses dwell on the certain reckoning and the transfer of the vineyard.

The Lord will come to judge. Henry's tradition reads the question as its own answer: "it is easy to say what, for nothing could be done more provoking." Ellicott notes a difference among the Evangelists: in Matthew the words "he will miserably destroy those wicked men" come from "the by-standers," while "here they form part of the parable itself" — Christ, perhaps, "having taken up and repeated" what others had said. The "destroying" the witnesses refer to the fall of the nation: it "was fulfilled," Henry's tradition says, "when Jerusalem was laid waste, and the Jewish nation extirpated"; Theophylact (on v. 1) names the agents, "the Romans."

The vineyard given "unto others" the witnesses read as the calling of the Gentiles. Clarke: "the vineyard must not perish with the husbandmen... I will give it into the care of new vine-dressers, the evangelists and apostles," under whose ministry "multitudes were brought to God before the destruction of Jerusalem." Theophylact (on v. 1) bids us "read the Acts of the Apostles, and you will find three thousand, and five thousand on a sudden believing and bearing fruit to God." Henry's tradition adds, "if he have not the rent from them, he will have it from another people, for God will be no loser by any" (cf. Colossians 1:6).

The witnesses thus find in the verse both judgment and hope. The judgment falls on the unfaithful tenants, whose long abuse of mercy ends at last in destruction; but the vineyard itself — the kingdom of God, with its privileges and its fruit — is not destroyed, only transferred to those who will render its fruit in season (cf. Jeremiah 17:3). The same parable that seals the doom of the husbandmen opens the door to the nations, so that the rejection of the Son by his own becomes, in the providence of God, the occasion of the gospel's passage to the whole world.
10:
"And have ye not read this scripture; The stone which the builders rejected is become the head of the corner:" The witnesses dwell on the citation, the builders, and the rejected stone.

Christ turns from parable to Scripture. Gill notes the question is aimed at men who "were, many of them, teachers of the people, and therefore ought to have read the scriptures, and... considered more especially such as respected the Messiah." The quotation is from Psalm 118:22 (Robertson, Wesley), introduced with a phrase used elsewhere for a weighty prophetic word now fulfilled (cf. Matthew 21:42).

The "builders" are the rulers, the same persons Christ had just called husbandmen. Bede (on v. 1) explains the change of figure: "the masters of the synagogue, whom He had just called husbandmen, He now calls builders, because the same persons who seemed to cultivate His people... were also commanded to construct and adorn this people, to be... a house worthy to have God for its inhabitant." The "stone which the builders rejected" is Christ, cast aside by those whose office was to build God's house. Lightfoot notes the Targum on this Psalm read it of "the child" — a striking witness, in Jewish tradition itself, to a messianic reference.

The "head of the corner" the witnesses read as Christ's exaltation and his uniting work. Theophylact (on v. 1) explains: "the stone which the builders refused, the same has become the head-stone of the corner, that is, of the Church; for the Church is, as it were, the corner, joining together Jews and Gentiles." The same stone the builders threw away as useless, God set at the head of his building, where two walls meet and are bonded into one. The witnesses thus find the citation the perfect seal of the parable: the rejected Son is the chosen corner-stone, and the very rejection that seemed to end his claim becomes, by God's hand, the foundation of a new and wider house. Robertson notes the formula "have ye not read" appeals to the written word as decisive (cf. Acts 1:16); the rulers, expert in the Scriptures, are confronted with a text from their own Psalter that pronounced their rejection of the stone and its divine reversal.
11:
"This was the Lord's doing, and it is marvellous in our eyes?" The witnesses dwell on the divine hand in Christ's exaltation and the wonder it evokes.

The clause continues the citation from the Psalm. Gill explains it of "the exaltation of the Messiah, after he had been so ill treated, and at last put to death by the Jews." Ellicott refines the rendering: "better, This was from the Lord," the pronoun being "in the feminine, agreeing with the head of the corner"; Robertson notes the feminine may reflect "the Hebrew original zoth (this thing)," a translation Hebraism. Either way, the meaning is that the whole work — the rejection of the stone and its setting at the head of the corner — was God's own doing.

The wonder belongs to the faithful. Theophylact (on v. 1) explains "marvellous in our eyes, that is, in the eyes of the faithful; for miracles meet with detraction from the faithless. The Church indeed is wonderful, as it were resting on wonders, for the Lord worked with the Apostles, and confirmed the word with signs." The exaltation of the rejected Christ, and the building of his Church upon him, is a marvel that faith adores and unbelief explains away.

Henry's tradition draws the application home. "The exaltation of Christ was the Lord's doing, and it is his doing to exalt him in our hearts, and to set up his throne there; and if it be done, it cannot but be marvellous in our eyes." The same God who raised the crucified Son to be the head of the corner works the like wonder in every believing heart, setting up there the throne the world rejected. The witnesses thus close the parable's argument on a note of adoring wonder: against all the contrivance of the builders, the stone they refused stands exalted by a hand they could not stay, and the sight of it — in the Church and in the soul — is, to those who see by faith, the marvellous doing of the Lord. Theophylact adds that the Church is "wonderful, as it were resting on wonders, for the Lord worked with the Apostles, and confirmed the word with signs" — so that the building raised upon the rejected stone is itself a standing miracle, the visible proof that the work was God's and not man's.
12:
"And they sought to lay hold on him, but feared the people: for they knew that he had spoken the parable against them: and they left him, and went their way." The witnesses dwell on the rulers' rage, their restraint, and their retreat.

They understood the parable and were enraged. Robertson notes "there was no mistaking the application, for he had specifically explained the application" (cf. Matthew 21:45); "the Sanhedrin were so angry that they actually started or sought to seize him." Ellicott traces the pronoun back to "the chief priests and scribes and elders" of Mark 11:18. Henry's tradition observes the irony that, in seeking to lay hold on him, they set about to fulfil the very thing the parable had foretold of them.

They were held back only by fear. "Nothing restrained them," Henry's tradition notes, "but the awe they stood in of the people; they did not reverence Christ, nor had any fear of God before their eyes, but were afraid... the mob would rise" (cf. John 7:30). Wesley marvels at the providence: "how wonderful is the providence of God, using all things for the good of his children! Generally the multitude is restrained from tearing them in pieces only by the fear of their rulers. And here the rulers themselves are restrained, through fear of the multitude!"

Their retreat the witnesses read as hardened impenitence. "They left him, and went their way," Henry's tradition explains: "if they could not do hurt to him, they resolved he should not do good to them, and therefore they got out of the hearing of his powerful preaching, lest they should be converted and healed." So the parable that was meant for their conviction served only to confirm them — for, the witnesses conclude, "if men's prejudices be not conquered by the evidence of truth, they are but confirmed; and if the gospel be not a savour of life unto life, it will be a savour of death unto death." The chapter's opening scene thus ends with the rulers more set against Christ than before, restrained from violence but unmelted by mercy.
13:
"And they send unto him certain of the Pharisees and of the Herodians, to catch him in his words." The witnesses dwell on the strange alliance, the design, and the snare.

The chief priests, unable to seize him themselves, work through others. Bede explains: "though they sought to take him, [they] feared the multitude, and therefore they endeavoured to effect what they could not do of themselves, by means of earthly powers, that they might themselves appear to be guiltless of his death." Henry's tradition marks the unnatural coalition: the Pharisees "great sticklers for the liberty of the Jews," the Herodians "for the Roman power" — "men that in this matter were contrary to one another, and yet concurred against Christ"; "it is no new thing for those that are at variance in other things to join in a confederacy against Christ" (cf. Mark 3:6).

The Herodians the fathers identify variously. Theophylact: either "a certain new heresy, who said that Herod was the Christ," or "the soldiers of Herod, whom the Pharisees brought as witnesses... that they might take him." Either way they were chosen to give the trap its teeth.

The design was to "catch him in his words." Robertson notes the verb (agreuō) is from "the hunt," to snare, while Matthew uses "to entrap" (cf. Matthew 22:15). The question, Bede explains, "was intended to induce him in his answer rather to fear God than Caesar, and to say that tribute should not be paid, so that the Herodians immediately on hearing it might hold him to be an author of sedition." Theophylact sets it as a dilemma with "a precipice on both sides": affirm the tribute, and "they might provoke the people against him"; deny it, and "they might accuse him, as though he was stirring up the people against Caesar." The witnesses thus see the scene as a calculated ambush — enemies sinking their mutual quarrels to combine against Christ, and framing a question designed so that any plain answer would destroy him. Robertson notes Matthew calls the agents "disciples" of the Pharisees and Luke "spies" (enkathetous), men suborned to feign sincerity; the smooth question was the bait of a hunt long planned against him.
14:
"Master, we know that thou art true, and carest for no man: for thou regardest not the person of men, but teachest the way of God in truth: Is it lawful to give tribute to Caesar, or not?" The witnesses dwell on the flattery, its self-condemning truth, and the question.

The approach was honeyed. Pseudo-Jerome pictures them as bees, who "carry honey in their mouth, but a sting in their tail"; Theophylact notes they "wished to deceive Christ by flattery." They call him "Master," own him "a Teacher of the way of God... in truth," one who "carest for no man" — that is, Ellicott explains, "thou regardest not the person of men," a phrase "essentially Hebrew in its form." The compliment was meant to commit him, before the people, to fearless speech.

Henry's tradition turns their flattery against them. "If they spoke as they thought concerning Christ, when they said, We know that thou art right, their persecuting him... was sin against knowledge; they knew him, and yet crucified him." "A man's testimony shall be taken most strongly against himself, and out of their own mouths are they judged": "they knew that he taught the way of God in truth, and yet rejected the counsel of God against themselves." If, on the other hand, they did not believe it, then "they lied unto God with their mouth, and flattered him with their tongue." Either way the praise condemns the praisers.

The question itself — "Is it lawful to give tribute to Caesar, or not?" — was, Henry's tradition notes, framed to "ensnare." They "seemed desirous to know their duty," even to "refer the determining of this matter to Christ," but "really they desired nothing but to know what he would say, in hopes that, which side soever he took... they might take occasion to accuse him." The witnesses observe how perilous it is to draw ministers into "controversies about civil rights"; yet the questioners' show of submission — "Shall we give, or shall we not give?" — only masked a settled malice that hoped for his ruin from whichever answer he gave.
15:
"Shall we give, or shall we not give? But he, knowing their hypocrisy, said unto them, Why tempt ye me? bring me a penny, that I may see it." The witnesses dwell on the pressing question, Christ's discernment, and his counter-move.

The repeated question presses for a practical verdict. Gill notes "they not only ask whether it was lawful, but whether also it was advisable to do it, that they might... charge him with persuading, or dissuading." Robertson marks that "Mark alone repeats the question in this sharp form" — the deliberative subjunctive, "shall we give or shall we not give?" — demanding a plain yes or no that would, they hoped, hang him either way.

Christ saw through them at once. Mark uses "the specific word that describes the sin of the questioners," Ellicott notes — "knowing their hypocrisy" — where Matthew has "wickedness" and Luke "craftiness." Robertson observes that "each of these words throws a flash-light on the spirit and attitude of these young men. They were sly, shrewd, slick, but they did not deceive Jesus with their pious palaver." Henry's tradition adds that "hypocrisy, though ever so artfully managed, cannot be concealed from the Lord Jesus": "he sees the potsherd that is covered with the silver dross."

His "Why tempt ye me?" lays bare their design, and his call for a penny turns the trap upon them. Henry's tradition explains that Christ "contrived the matter so as to ensnare them, and to oblige them by their own words to do what they were unwilling to do" — to acknowledge, by producing the coin, the very Roman authority they pretended to question. By asking to see the denarius, the witnesses observe, Christ shifts the ground from a theoretical debate to a plain fact: the coin in their own purses would answer the question they had hoped would destroy him. The hunters are quietly led into their own snare, made to furnish, from their pockets, the proof that will silence them. Gill notes some copies omit the doubled "shall we give, or not give?", but the urgency is plain — they pressed for a verdict not merely on the law but on the policy, hoping to charge him as one who either persuaded or dissuaded the payment of tribute.
16:
"And they brought it. And he saith unto them, Whose is this image and superscription? And they said unto him, Caesar's." The witnesses dwell on the coin, its image, and the wisdom of the question.

The coin was the Roman denarius. Gill notes "the penny, which was a Roman one, and worth seven pence halfpenny of our money"; Bede (on v. 13) describes it as "a piece of money... bearing the image of Caesar." Lightfoot adds rabbinic detail, distinguishing "a Caesarean penny" from "Jerusalem money," and the silver penny from the gold (the gold worth twenty-five of silver) — the very currency in circulation among the Jews bore the emperor's stamp.

The "image and superscription" — better "inscription" (Ellicott) — the witnesses make the hinge of the answer. By asking whose they were, and drawing from the questioners' own lips the answer "Caesar's," Christ established a fact they could not deny: they used, and therefore acknowledged, Caesar's coinage. Bede guards against misreading the question as ignorance: "let those who think that our Saviour asked the question through ignorance and not by an economy learn from this that he might have known whose image it was; but he puts the question in order to return them a fitting answer."

The witnesses thus find in this brief exchange the quiet masterstroke of the whole encounter. The current coin of the realm, stamped with Caesar's image and name, was itself the proof that the Jews lived under, and benefited from, Roman government. To carry and use such money was to confess the authority that issued it. Christ does not lecture them on the rights of princes; he simply makes them hold up the coin and name its owner. Their own answer — "Caesar's" — supplies the premise from which his conclusion will follow, so that the verdict, when it comes, will rest not on his bare word but on their own admission. The trap is sprung, and the bait is the questioners' own penny. Ellicott notes the word is better rendered "inscription" than "superscription", the legend running round the coin beside the emperor's head; that legend and image together were the silent witness, carried in every Jewish purse, of the authority under which they lived.
17:
"And Jesus answering said unto them, Render to Caesar the things that are Caesar's, and to God the things that are God's. And they marvelled at him." The witnesses dwell on the twofold rendering and the amazement it produced.

The principle is to give to each his own. Theophylact paraphrases: "Give what bears an image to him whose image it bears, that is, the penny to Caesar; for we can both pay Caesar his tribute, and offer to God what is his own." Henry's tradition draws the balance: "Caesar might command their money for the public benefit," for "the circulation of the money is from him"; yet "Caesar might not command their consciences" — "Render to Caesar the things that are Caesar's, but be sure to render to God the things that are God's." Barnes gathers the apostolic echoes (Romans 13:7; 1 Peter 2:17).

The "things that are God's" the witnesses fill out. Bede names "tithes, first-fruits, oblations, and victims"; but the deeper rendering is of ourselves. Pseudo-Jerome: "render yourselves willingly up to God, for the light of thy countenance, O Lord (Psalm 4:6), and not of Caesar's, is stamped upon us." Henry's tradition presses the same: "our hearts and best affections are as much due to him as ever rent was to a landlord, or tribute to a prince" — and perhaps Christ "referred to the parable he had just now put forth," in which they had failed to render the fruits to the Lord of the vineyard (cf. Malachi 1:6).

The answer left them amazed. Robertson notes Mark's imperfect "they marvelled greatly at him," while Luke adds they "held their peace" and Matthew that they "went their way." Wesley: "they marvelled at the wisdom of his answer." Yet Henry's tradition observes the sad limit of their wonder: "all that heard Christ marvelled at the discretion of his answer... but I doubt none were brought by it, as they ought to be, to render to God themselves and their devotions" — "many will commend the wit of a sermon, that will not be commanded by the divine laws of a sermon." The witnesses thus find in the saying both a charter for the Christian's double duty — honest subjection to lawful government and whole-hearted devotion to God — and a mirror of the human heart, quick to admire wisdom it will not obey.
18:
"Then come unto him the Sadducees, which say there is no resurrection; and they asked him, saying," The witnesses identify the sect, their unbelief, and their motive in coming.

The Sadducees were the sceptics of the age. Theophylact calls them "a certain heretical sect of the Jews [who] denied the resurrection, and said that there was neither angel nor spirit" (cf. Acts 23:8); the Gloss adds that "they contradicted the Scriptures, and derogated from the power of God." Henry's tradition describes them as "the deists of that age," not "bigots and persecutors" like the Pharisees, but "sceptics and infidels," whose "design was upon his doctrine, to hinder the spreading of" the great truths "of the resurrection, and world of spirits, and a state of rewards and punishments."

Their coming, Robertson notes, followed the rout of the Sanhedrin's committee, the Pharisees, and the Herodians: "it was inevitable that they should feel called upon to show their intellectual superiority to these raw Pharisaic and Herodian theologians," airing "their disbelief in the resurrection at the expense of the Pharisees" and hoping "to score against Jesus where" the others "had failed so ignominiously."

Pseudo-Jerome reads the coming tale mystically: the woman who buries seven husbands and leaves no seed is "the Jewish synagogue, deserted by the seven-fold Spirit," to whom the patriarchs "did not leave... the seed of Abraham, that is, Jesus Christ," who "was given to us Gentiles." The witnesses thus set the stage for the third assault: where the Pharisees had attacked his loyalty and the Herodians his politics, the Sadducees now attack his teaching at its foundation, framing a tale meant to make the resurrection look absurd — and so, unwittingly, to give the Lord occasion to establish, from their own accepted Scriptures, the very truth they denied. Henry's tradition notes that these "great and fundamental truths which they denied, Christ had made it his business to establish and prove, and had carried the notion of them much further than ever it was before"; so they "set themselves to perplex his doctrine," choosing the ground where they felt strongest and where the Pharisees had so often been baffled.
19:
"Master, Moses wrote unto us, If a man's brother die, and leave his wife behind him, and leave no children, that his brother should take his wife, and raise up seed unto his brother." The witnesses identify the law cited and the Sadducees' use of Scripture.

The law is the levirate. Robertson notes "Moses wrote" refers to Deuteronomy 25:5 (and the precedent of Genesis 38:8), the ordinance "that, if a man died without issue, his brother was obliged to marry his widow" (Henry's tradition). Barnes adds the cross-reference to Ruth. The point of the law was the preservation of a man's name and inheritance in Israel, that he should not be blotted out for want of an heir.

The Sadducees' appeal to Moses is itself significant. Gill notes "they acknowledged the writings of Moses, and indeed all the Scriptures of the Old Testament, adhering to the literal sense of them, and rejecting the traditional interpretation... by the Rabbins." It was on the ground of Scripture, then — and chiefly the books of Moses, which they held in highest authority — that they framed their challenge; and it is from Moses, fittingly, that Christ will answer them.

Henry's tradition observes that "the method they take to entangle" his doctrine is to "quote the ancient law" and build upon it a case. The law, good and wise in its own purpose, they would turn into a weapon against the hope of the resurrection — supposing that the family relation it provided for must, if there be a future life, create an insoluble tangle there. The witnesses thus see the Sadducees beginning, as cunning adversaries do, from common ground: a statute their opponent must own, from which they think to draw an absurdity. Their reverence for Moses is real enough as far as it goes; their error lies in reading his law with no eye to the power and purpose of the God who gave it. Wesley and Robertson note that Mark, like Luke, writes "Moses wrote" where Matthew has "Moses said," pointing to the written ordinance as the fixed ground of the appeal; the Sadducees stand on the letter of the text, but it is the letter that will be turned against them.
20:
"Now there were seven brethren: and the first took a wife, and dying left no seed." The witnesses dwell on the framing of the hypothetical and the design behind it.

The case is a supposed one, built to press the law to an extreme. Gill notes the "seven brethren" are imagined "in a certain family," brethren "by the father's side; for such only were reckoned so, and such only did this law oblige." The number seven is chosen, Henry's tradition observes, to multiply the difficulty: the Sadducees "suppose a case to happen that, according to that law, seven brothers were, successively, the husbands of one woman."

The design behind the framing was twofold. First, Henry's tradition suggests, "these Sadducees, according to their wonted profaneness, intended hereby to ridicule that law, and so to bring the whole frame of the Mosaic institution into contempt, as absurd and inconvenient in the practice of it" — for "those who deny divine truths, commonly set themselves to disparage divine laws and ordinances." Second, and chiefly, the case was meant "to expose the doctrine of the resurrection; for they suppose that if there be a future state, it must be such a one as this."

The witnesses thus see in the opening of the tale the careful construction of a trap. The first brother marries and dies childless, the law requiring the next to take the widow; and so the stage is set for the chain of marriages that follows. There is nothing in the case itself impossible or even improbable — "such a thing," Bede allows, "might really have happened at some time or other among them." The cunning lies not in the facts supposed but in the conclusion the Sadducees mean to draw from them: that a doctrine which would leave one woman the wife of seven men must be self-evidently absurd. They build their snare brick by brick, beginning with a single childless death, and trusting that the plain law of Moses will lead, by its own working, to a manifest contradiction in the hope of the resurrection. Robertson notes Mark's plain "took a wife" (elaben gynaika), where Matthew has "married"; the homely directness suits a tale told for argument, each clause advancing the supposed history toward the question the Sadducees mean to spring.
21:
"And the second took her, and died, neither left he any seed: and the third likewise." The witnesses follow the unfolding of the case under the levirate canon.

The chain proceeds by the law itself. Gill notes that "the second took her to wife, married her, as the next eldest brother, by the above law was obliged to" — each surviving brother, in turn, bound by the ordinance of Deuteronomy 25:5 to take the childless widow and seek to raise up seed to the dead. The repetition — "and the third likewise" — carries the supposed case forward through brother after brother, each marrying in obedience to Moses, each dying without issue.

The rabbinic canons, Gill observes, regulated such a succession in detail, so that the Sadducees' tale, however contrived, ran along lines their hearers would recognise as lawful. There was no breach of the statute in it; on the contrary, each marriage was precisely what the law required. The very faithfulness of the brothers to the levirate command is what builds the difficulty: it is by keeping Moses that the woman comes to have had so many husbands.

The witnesses thus see the case advancing with a kind of grim inevitability. Henry's tradition had noted the Sadducees' aim — to clog the doctrine of the resurrection "either with this invincible absurdity, that a woman in that state must have seven husbands, or else with this insolvable difficulty, whose wife must she be." Each added brother tightens the supposed knot. The adversaries spin out the tale deliberately, dwelling on the second and the third and so on, not because the facts are remarkable but because the accumulation is essential to their argument: the more husbands the woman lawfully had, the more impossible, they think, to assign her to any one of them in a risen life. So the snare is drawn tighter with every step, and the hearer is led on toward the question the Sadducees believe no defender of the resurrection can answer. Gill notes the rabbinic rulings even prescribed which brother was bound and in what order, so that the supposed succession was not fanciful but a faithful picture of how the law was understood to operate; the Sadducees take care that no flaw in the telling should give Christ an opening.
22:
"And the seven had her, and left no seed: last of all the woman died also." The witnesses note the completion of the case and the absurdity it was meant to fix.

The tale reaches its end. Gill sums it: "all the seven brethren married her, one after another, and neither of them had any children by her"; and then, "last of all," the woman herself died. Robertson notes the adverbial "last of all" ( eschaton pantōn) closing the recital. The whole supposed history is now complete: one woman, seven successive husbands, no offspring, and at length her own death — every detail laid in place for the question to follow.

The point of the completed case is the difficulty it was framed to create. Henry's tradition states it: the Sadducees thought the doctrine of a future state "clogged either with this invincible absurdity, that a woman in that state must have seven husbands, or else with this insolvable difficulty, whose wife must she be." With all seven dead childless and the woman dead too, there is no natural heir to settle the claim, and so — they suppose — no way to assign her in the resurrection without either polygamy or an impossible choice.

The witnesses thus see the snare fully set. The Sadducees have built, out of a lawful application of Moses, a scenario they believe reduces the resurrection to nonsense. Their confidence, Henry's tradition notes, is the confidence of men who "do not deny the truth, nor say, There can be no resurrection," but feign to accept it and ask only for instruction, "when really they are designing to give a fatal stab, and think that they shall do it." The completed tale is their masterstroke, as they imagine — a single woman whose seven marriages, all required by God's own law, seem to make the hope of the risen life collapse under its own weight. It remains only to put the question, and wait, as they think, for the Teacher's defeat. Gill observes that "neither of them had any children by her," so that the levirate purpose — to raise up seed to the dead — wholly failed through all seven; the law had run its full course and produced only the tangle the Sadducees now lay before him.
23:
"In the resurrection therefore, when they shall rise, whose wife shall she be of them? for the seven had her to wife." The witnesses dwell on the question itself and the cunning behind it.

The question is the point toward which the whole tale has been built. Gill notes the phrase "when they shall rise" is omitted in some copies and versions, "because, perhaps, it might be thought superfluous," and Clarke records the same textual doubt; but the sense is plain — supposing a resurrection, and supposing these seven and their wife to rise, "whose wife shall she be of them?" Robertson notes the predicate accusative "to wife," as in Luke.

The cunning lies in the framing. Henry's tradition marks how subtly "these heretics undermine the truth": "they do not deny it, nor say, There can be no resurrection; nay, they do not seem to doubt of it." Rather, "they take it for granted that there is a resurrection, and would be thought to desire instruction concerning it, when really they are designing to give a fatal stab." They assume that a risen life must simply continue the relations of this one, so that the woman must be wife to some one of the seven — and since she cannot in fairness be wife to all, nor wife to one more than another, the doctrine, they think, is caught in an inescapable contradiction.

The witnesses thus identify the hidden flaw the Lord will expose. The whole force of the question rests on a false assumption: that the resurrection world is governed by the same fleshly relations as this. "It is the common artifice of heretics and Sadducees," Henry's tradition observes, "to perplex and entangle the truth which they have not the impudence to deny." Their question is not a sincere request but a baited hook, dressed in the form of an honest difficulty. And its very ingenuity is its undoing, for it betrays, as Christ will show, a double ignorance — of the Scriptures, which never promised a merely earthly future, and of the power of God, who raises men to a life beyond the need and the bond of marriage.
24:
"And Jesus answering said unto them, Do ye not therefore err, because ye know not the scriptures, neither the power of God?" The witnesses dwell on the double charge that exposes the root of the Sadducees' error.

Christ answers their question with a diagnosis. Robertson notes "Mark puts it as a question with ou expecting the affirmative answer" — "do ye not err?" — where Matthew "puts it as a positive assertion: Ye are." The verb (planaomai), Robertson adds, is "to wander astray," like the Latin erro. Ellicott renders the participle "not knowing the scriptures."

The first cause of their error is ignorance of Scripture. They had read it, but, Henry's tradition explains, "they did not know the sense and meaning of them, but put false constructions upon them; or they did not receive the scriptures as the word of God, but set up their own corrupt reasonings in opposition." Robertson notes the irony: "the Sadducees posed as men of superior intelligence... and yet on this very point they were ignorant of the Scriptures. How much error today is due to this same ignorance among the educated!" (cf. 1 Corinthians 15:34). "A right knowledge of the scripture," Henry's tradition adds, "is the best preservative against error."

The second cause is ignorance of God's power. They "could not but know that God is almighty," Henry's tradition observes, "but they would not apply that doctrine to this matter," surrendering "the truth to the objections of the impossibility of it." Yet "the same power that made soul and body... can preserve the body safe, and the soul active, when they are parted, and can unite them together again" (cf. Romans 4:21). Robertson notes "the two kinds of ignorance generally go together." The witnesses thus find Christ tracing the Sadducees' clever puzzle to its source: not a real difficulty in the doctrine, but a twofold blindness in themselves — they neither understood what God had revealed nor reckoned with what God could do. Ellicott notes the participial form, "not knowing the scriptures," makes the ignorance the very cause of the wandering; the men who prided themselves on reason had reasoned themselves out of both the word and the power of God, the two pillars on which the hope of the resurrection rests.
25:
"For when they shall rise from the dead, they neither marry, nor are given in marriage; but are as the angels which are in heaven." The witnesses dwell on the nature of the risen state and the answer it gives to the Sadducees' puzzle.

The risen life knows no marriage. Theophylact (on v. 18) explains: "there will be a certain heavenly and angelic restoration to life, when there shall be no more decay, and we shall remain unchanged; and for this reason marriage shall cease. For marriage now exists on account of our decay, that we may be carried on by succession of our race, and not fail; but then we shall be as the Angels, who need no succession by marriage." Henry's tradition adds that "the relation between husband and wife, though instituted in the earthly paradise, will not be known in the heavenly one," so "it is a folly to ask, Whose wife shall she be?"

"As the angels in heaven" the commentators read with care. Barnes notes the likeness is "in respect to connexions and relations": "the peculiar relation of marriage will not exist," though the words do not affirm "that there will be no recollection of former marriages, or no recognition of each other." Robertson cites Luke's "equal unto the angels," the equality consisting, Swete says, "in their deliverance from mortality and its consequences," since "the angels are directly created, not procreated."

The witnesses thus find the Sadducees' whole difficulty dissolved by a true view of the future state. Their error, Henry's tradition observes, was to "measure our ideas of the world of spirits by the affairs of this world of sense" — to expect, like "Turks and infidels... sensual pleasures in their fools' paradise." But "flesh and blood shall not inherit the kingdom of God" (1 Corinthians 15:50); the risen saints "expect better things — even a full satisfaction in God's love and likeness" (cf. Psalm 17:15). The question "whose wife?" has no answer because it has no place: in the deathless life of the resurrection, the bond made for a dying world has served its end and ceases (cf. Luke 20:36).
26:
"And as touching the dead, that they rise: have ye not read in the book of Moses, how in the bush God spake unto him, saying, I am the God of Abraham, and the God of Isaac, and the God of Jacob?" The witnesses dwell on the proof drawn from Moses himself.

Christ meets the Sadducees on their own ground. They prized the books of Moses above all; so from Moses he proves the resurrection. Cyril of Jerusalem notes the like need with the Samaritans, "who, receiving the Law only, allow not the Prophets": to such "let us go to the writings of the Law," where "God says to Moses, I am the God of Abraham, and of Isaac, and of Jacob" — words that "must mean of those who have being and subsistence."

"At the bush" the commentators explain as a citation by heading. Ellicott notes the phrase points "not as the place in which God spoke, but as the title or heading by which the section was commonly described"; Robertson agrees it is "the technical use" — "in the passage about the Bush" (Exodus 3:6) — for "when there were no chapters and verses, it was the easiest way of quoting a book of the Old Testament by the subject" (Barnes).

The weight of the proof lies in the present tense. Theophylact (on v. 18) had marked it: God "did not say, I have been, but I am, as if they had been present." Long after Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob had died, God still called himself their God — not "I was" but "I am" their God. The witnesses thus see Christ resting the resurrection on a single word of Scripture the Sadducees could not reject. If God remains the God of the patriarchs, then the patriarchs, in some real sense, still live to him; and a relation so solemnly avowed by the living God with men long dead points beyond the grave to a life that death has not ended — the very truth the Sadducees had come to deny, established from the very Lawgiver they revered.
27:
"He is not the God of the dead, but the God of the living: ye therefore do greatly err." The witnesses unfold the inference and the solemn close.

The inference is that the patriarchs live. Gill draws it: since "God is the God of these persons, they must be now alive in their souls, for God is not the God of the dead; and... their bodies must rise again, or he will not be the God of their whole persons." Wesley sets out the full argument: "since the character of his being the God of any persons plainly intimates a relation to them, not as dead, but as living"; and since God "cannot be said to be at present their God at all, if they are utterly dead," nor "the God of human persons... if their bodies were to abide in everlasting death; there must needs be a future state of blessedness, and a resurrection of the body."

Henry's tradition fills out both halves of the conclusion. It is "absurd to think that God's relation to Abraham should be... solemnly recognised, if Abraham was annihilated, or that the living God should be the portion and happiness of a man that is dead"; therefore Abraham's soul "exists and acts," and "therefore, some time or other, the body must rise again," for there is "such an innate inclination in a human soul towards its body, as would make a total and everlasting separation inconsistent with the bliss... of those souls that have the Lord for their God."

The close is solemn but kindly. "Ye therefore do greatly err," found only in Mark, Robertson calls a "solemn, severe, impressive, but kindly close." (Clarke notes the word "God" is omitted in many manuscripts of the final clause.) The witnesses thus see the Sadducees answered out of their own Scriptures and confounded in their own subtlety: those who "deny the resurrection greatly err, and ought to be told so" (Henry's tradition). The God of the living has bound himself to Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob by an everlasting covenant, and that covenant is itself the pledge that they live, and shall rise — a hope, the witnesses add, that bids us "pass through this dying world with a joyful hope of eternal happiness, and of a glorious resurrection."
28:
"And one of the scribes came, and having heard them reasoning together, and perceiving that he had answered them well, asked him, Which is the first commandment of all?" The witnesses dwell on this scribe's better spirit and the weight of his question.

Mark presents him favourably. Robertson notes Mark "puts this scribe in a favourable light, knowing that he had answered them well" — "them" being the Sadducees. Matthew, however, says the lawyer was "tempting" him (cf. Matthew 22:36). Augustine reconciles the two: "though he came as a tempter, yet he was corrected by the answer of the Lord," or the "temptation" was no evil intent but "the caution of a man who wished to try a thing unknown to him." Henry's tradition marks his civility: he "took notice of Christ's answer to the Sadducees, and owned that he had answered well," and "his application to Christ for instruction... became him; not tempting Christ, but desiring to improve his acquaintance."

The question concerns the chief commandment. By "first," Henry's tradition explains, he means "not the first in order, but the first in weight and dignity" — "that command... our obedience to which will lay a foundation for our obedience to all the rest." Robertson notes Matthew has "great" rather than "first," but the sense is one.

Behind the question lay a standing rabbinic debate. Lightfoot notes the Rabbis' distinction "between the law and the precept," and that the scribe asks whether the great ceremonial precepts — "sacrifices, purifications, keeping festivals" — were the greatest, "or no." Bede confirms "a grave question was often debated between the scribes and Pharisees, which was the first commandment": "some praised offerings and sacrifices, others preferred acts of faith and love." The witnesses thus see the scribe raising, in better faith than his fellows, the very question on which the heart of true religion turns — and bringing it, fittingly, to the one able to answer it. Henry's tradition notes that "not that any commandment of God is little — they are all the commands of a great God — but some are greater than others, moral precepts than rituals"; the scribe asks not which may be neglected but which is supreme, the command to which all the rest look up.
29:
"And Jesus answered him, The first of all the commandments is, Hear, O Israel; The Lord our God is one Lord:" The witnesses dwell on the great confession on which the first commandment rests.

Christ answers by laying a doctrinal foundation before the duty. Mark, Henry's tradition notes, "prefixes to this command the great doctrinal truth upon which it is built": "Hear, O Israel, the Lord our God is one Lord; if we firmly believe this, it will follow that we shall love him with all our heart." Barnes renders it literally, "Jehovah, our God, is one Jehovah" — the truth that "the God of the Jews was one, and one only," the very purpose "of the separation of the Jewish people from other nations to keep in mind" (cf. Deuteronomy 6:4).

These words were Israel's daily creed. Ellicott notes that "the opening words... were in common use under the name of the Shemà (the Hebrew for Hear), and formed the popular expression of the faith of Israel" — "to say the Shemà was a passport into Paradise for any child of Abraham." Mark, he observes, "gives the quotation more fully than St. Matthew," keeping the great confession of God's unity.

From that unity the duty of whole-hearted love directly follows. Wesley draws the inference: "The Lord our God... is one God, essentially, though three persons. From this unity of God it follows that we owe all our love to him alone." Henry's tradition agrees: "he is one Lord, therefore he must be loved with our whole heart; he has the sole right to us, and therefore ought to have the sole possession of us." The witnesses thus see Christ grounding the first commandment not in arbitrary decree but in the nature of God himself: because the Lord is one, undivided, and ours, the heart that knows him owes him an undivided love, admitting no rival to his throne (cf. Luke 10:27). Henry's tradition adds that "he is Jehovah, who has all amiable perfections in himself; he is our God, to whom we stand related and obliged; and therefore we ought to love him, to set our affections on him" — the unity, the perfection, and the covenant nearness of God together making whole-hearted love both reasonable and due.
30:
"And thou shalt love the Lord thy God with all thy heart, and with all thy soul, and with all thy mind, and with all thy strength: this is the first commandment." The witnesses dwell on the totality of the love commanded and the powers it engages.

Every faculty is claimed for God. Theophylact (on v. 28) reads the clauses as an inventory of the soul's powers: "there is a living power in the soul... to this belong anger and desire," given to God in "with all thy soul"; "another power, which is called natural, to which belong nutriment and growth," given in "with all thy heart"; "another power, the rational, which he calls the mind, and that too is to be given whole to God." Wesley sums "with all thy strength" as "the whole strength and capacity of thy understanding, will, and affections" (cf. Deuteronomy 6:5).

This love is the master-principle of the renewed soul. Henry's tradition explains why it is "the great commandment of all, which is indeed inclusive of all": "love is the leading affection of the soul; the love of God is the leading grace in the renewed soul." "Where there is a commanding principle in the soul, there is a disposition to every other duty"; and "where this is not, nothing else that is good is done, or done aright, or accepted, or done long."

The witnesses press both the obligation and the means. Gill notes that though "there is now no principle of love to God in men... this commandment is still in force; the fall of man, the corruption of nature... do not make it null and void"; rather, "in regeneration, when God puts his laws into the heart," love to God is "produced." So the command both convicts and directs: it shows the heart its bound duty, which no corruption cancels, and points it to the grace by which alone that duty can be fulfilled. To love God with the whole man, the witnesses conclude, is not one duty among many but the spring of all, the commanding affection that, once enthroned, makes every other commandment no longer grievous but the welcome expression of a heart wholly his.
31:
"And the second is like, namely this, Thou shalt love thy neighbour as thyself. There is none other commandment greater than these." The witnesses dwell on the second commandment and its bond with the first.

The second resembles the first and flows from it. Theophylact (on v. 28) explains why Christ says it is "like": "these two commandments are harmonious one with the other, and mutually contain the other. For he who loves God loves also his creature; but the chief of his creatures is man, wherefore he who loves God ought to love all men." Wesley calls it "of a like comprehensive nature, comprising our whole duty to man" (cf. Leviticus 19:18; Romans 13:9). Ellicott notes the better manuscripts read simply "the second is this," omitting "like."

The measure of this love is self-love. Henry's tradition: we must love our neighbour "as truly and sincerely as we love ourselves, and in the same instances, and we must show it by doing as we would be done by." And the ground is shared nature: "we must love our neighbour as ourselves, because he is of the same nature with ourselves... of one body, of one society"; and "if a fellow-Christian, and of the same sacred society, the obligation is the stronger" — "hath not one God created us?"

Together the two commandments comprehend the whole law. "There is none other commandment greater than these," Christ declares, "for," Henry's tradition explains, "in these all the law is fulfilled, and if we make conscience of obedience to these, all other instances of obedience will follow of course" (cf. Matthew 22:40, "on these hang all the law and the prophets"). The witnesses thus see the second commandment not as a rival to the first but as its overflow: the love that rises to God descends to the neighbour, and the two together — God above all, the neighbour as oneself — gather into one root every particular duty the law commands. Henry's tradition presses the second's ground further: "hath not one God created us? has not one Christ redeemed us?" — so that the bond of a common Maker, and for believers a common Redeemer, lays on us the obligation to love our neighbour as a partaker with us of the same nature and the same grace.
32:
"And the scribe said unto him, Well, Master, thou hast said the truth: for there is one God; and there is none other but he:" The witnesses dwell on the scribe's hearty assent, peculiar to Mark.

This reply, the commentators note, is Mark's alone. Gill, Clarke, and Barnes all remark "this answer of the scribe is not found in Matthew" — Clarke counting it "another proof against Mark's supposed abridgment." Far from condensing, Mark preserves a striking exchange the others omit.

The scribe's words breathe sincere admiration. Ellicott observes that "Well, Master, thou hast said the truth" is "intentionally repeated from Mark 12:14, but uttered now, not with the covert sneer of the hypocrite, but in the sincerity of admiration." The address "Master" — "Teacher, Rabbi" — shows "real reverence": "he recognises the speaker as one of his own order." Robertson takes the "well" (kalōs) as "exclamatory": "Fine, Teacher. Of a truth didst thou say." The whole, Ellicott adds, is "an addition of singular interest, as showing the existence among the scribes of some who accepted our Lord's teaching... and were able to distinguish between its essence and its accidents."

Calvin weighs the scribe's character. He "alone" of the questioners "was softened down"; and "though he had attacked Christ maliciously... not only does he silently yield, but openly and candidly assents." "Thus we see that he did not belong to the class of those enemies whose obstinacy is incurable." His confession that "there is one God, and there is none other but he" (cf. Deuteronomy 4:39) Calvin attributes to "the seed of right knowledge" which "lay choked in his heart" until Christ's word drew it out. The witnesses thus find in the scribe a hopeful exception among the hostile rulers — a man whose better understanding, long buried under the ceremonialism of his class, awakens at the touch of the truth and answers it with candid, reverent agreement. Henry's tradition observes that, though "Christ's assertions needed not the scribe's attestations", yet his open confession "shall be brought in evidence against those who persecuted Christ as a deceiver, that one of themselves, even a scribe of their own, confessed that he said the truth."
33:
"And to love him with all the heart, and with all the understanding, and with all the soul, and with all the strength, and to love his neighbour as himself, is more than all whole burnt offerings and sacrifices." The witnesses dwell on the scribe's insight and the prophetic truth behind it.

The scribe presses love above sacrifice. Henry's tradition notes he not only assents but explains: love to God is "loving him with the understanding, as those that know what abundant reason we have to love him" — "our love to God, as it must be an entire, so it must be an intelligent love." And he ranks this love "more than all whole-burnt-offerings and sacrifices, more acceptable to God." Ellicott hears "a fervour in the eloquence of the scribe's answer which indicates the earnestness, almost the enthusiasm, of conviction"; such teaching as 1 Samuel 15:22, Hosea 6:6, and Micah 6:6 "had not been in vain for him."

Calvin guards the comparison from misuse. It might seem "incongruous that sacrifices, which... belong to the first table of the Law, should be reckoned of less importance than charity towards men." His reply: "though the worship of God is greatly preferable... yet its outward exercises ought not to be estimated so highly as to swallow up brotherly kindness." Moreover "it is naked and empty sacrifices that are here spoken of," for "our Lord contrasts a hypocritical appearance of piety with true and sincere uprightness."

The witnesses thus find the scribe voicing a truth the prophets had long taught: that God is "not appeased by offerings of beasts, where brotherly kindness is neglected" (Calvin). The sacrifices, Henry's tradition adds, "only represented the atonements for men's transgressions... and led to moral obedience"; love is the substance they shadowed. To prefer love to sacrifice is not to despise God's appointed worship but to grasp its purpose — that the outward rite was always meant to express and serve the inward devotion of a heart given to God and a hand open to the neighbour. In owning this, the scribe shows himself near to the very heart of the law.
34:
"And when Jesus saw that he answered discreetly, he said unto him, Thou art not far from the kingdom of God. And no man after that durst ask him any question." The witnesses dwell on Christ's commendation, its limit, and the silencing of his foes.

The scribe answered with his wits about him. Robertson explains "discreetly" (nounechōs, from mind and have) means "using the mind to good effect... he had his wits about him." Henry's tradition is glad of it after "so many even of the scribes... that answered indiscreetly": he replied "as a rational intelligent man... one that had considered."

Christ's "not far from the kingdom" is real praise, yet measured. Theophylact (on v. 28) notes its limit: "he did not say, Thou art within the kingdom of heaven, but, Thou art not far." Bede explains the nearness: he "proved himself to be a favourer of that opinion which is proper to the New Testament and to Gospel perfection." Ellicott draws out the principle: "the recognition of that righteousness as consisting in the fulfilment of the two commandments... brought a man to the very threshold of the Kingdom" — contrasting the lawyer of Luke 10:27 who "minimised the force of the commandments by his narrowing definitions." Clarke hopes "he did at last believe in and confess Jesus."

The encouragement, Calvin notes, teaches us to "assist those in whom there is any beginning either of docility or of right understanding"; "in his person Christ encourages us all, after having once entered into the right path, to proceed." Wesley turns it on the reader: "art not thou? then go on: be a real Christian." The scene then closes the long contest: "no man after that durst ask him any question" (cf. Matthew 22:46). Robertson notes the double negative and the imperfect "dared": "the debate was closed... Jesus was complete victor on every side." Yet Calvin warns the silence was not surrender — the foes "groaned within, like wild beasts shut up in their dens." The witnesses thus end the day's disputes with Christ triumphant over every party, and one honest scribe left standing at the threshold of the kingdom.
35:
"And Jesus answered and said, while he taught in the temple, How say the scribes that Christ is the Son of David?" The witnesses dwell on Christ's counter-question and the truth it was meant to draw out.

The questioners silenced, Christ now questions them. Robertson notes that, though "they no longer dare to question Jesus, he has one to put to them" as he goes on "teaching in the temple" (a detail, Ellicott observes, "named by St. Mark only"). It is no mere puzzle: "He contents himself with pointing out a difficulty, in the solution of which lay the key to the whole problem of His person and work" (Swete).

The scribes' teaching was true but incomplete. They held, rightly, that "the Messiah was to be the Son of David" (cf. Luke 1:32; John 7:42). But Theophylact notes they "said that Christ was the Son of David, not his Lord," and Bede that the Jews "assert that he is a mere man, a holy Person descended from David." Robertson: "the rabbis had overlooked the fact that David in Psalm 110:1 called the Messiah his Lord also. The deity and the humanity of the Messiah are both involved."

Henry's tradition draws out the scribes' failure as teachers. "They had taught the people that... he should be a branch of their royal family; but they had not taught them that which was for the honour of the Messiah himself — that he should be the Son of God, and, as such, David's Lord." "Those are unworthy to sit in Moses's seat who, though they are able to preach the truth, are not in some measure able to defend it." The witnesses thus see Christ exposing not an error but a half-truth: the scribes proclaimed the Messiah's descent and missed his divinity, and so could not answer how David's son could be David's Lord — a question whose only answer is the mystery of the incarnate Son of God. Pseudo-Jerome adds that Christ "openly speaks to them of himself, that they may be inexcusable"; the question was not merely instructive but probing, leaving the learned rulers without defence for the poverty of their teaching about the Christ.
36:
"For David himself said by the Holy Ghost, The LORD said to my Lord, Sit thou on my right hand, till I make thine enemies thy footstool." The witnesses dwell on the inspiration of the words and the Lordship they ascribe to the Messiah.

The quotation is from Psalm 110:1, and Mark stresses its inspiration. Ellicott notes "St. Mark is more emphatic in ascribing the words of David to the influence of the Holy Spirit than either St. Matthew, who simply quotes, or St. Luke, who uses the more general phrase in spirit" (cf. 2 Peter 1:21; 2 Samuel 23:2). Theophylact (on v. 35) presses the point: "ye cannot say that David said this without the grace of the Holy Spirit, but he called him Lord in the Holy Spirit."

The words declare the Messiah's session and triumph. "The Lord said to my Lord, Sit thou on my right hand" — David, by the Spirit, hears Jehovah address one whom David himself owns as "my Lord." The promise "till I make thine enemies thy footstool," Theophylact explains, proves his Lordship, "for they themselves were his enemies, whom God put under the footstool of Christ." (Robertson notes a textual variant, "underneath" for "footstool.")

Cyril of Jerusalem gathers the apostolic witness to this session at God's right hand: Christ's own word that "ye shall see the Son of Man sitting on the right hand of power," Peter's that he "is on the right hand of God, having gone into heaven," and Paul's repeated testimony — that God "set him at his own right hand," that the risen "seek the things above, where Christ is seated at the right hand of God." The witnesses thus find in David's inspired word the very truth the scribes had missed: the Messiah is no mere son of David but One whom David, speaking by the Holy Ghost, calls Lord — exalted to the throne of God and awaiting the subjection of all his enemies. Bede notes the Father's subduing of the enemies "does not shew the weakness of the Son, but the unity of nature, by which One works in the Other; for the Son also subjects the Father's enemies"; the session at the right hand bespeaks not a lesser dignity but the shared working of Father and Son.
37:
"David therefore himself calleth him Lord; and whence is he then his son? And the common people heard him gladly." The witnesses dwell on the dilemma posed and the gladness of the crowd.

The dilemma is unanswerable on the scribes' premises. Gill puts it: "David, whose son you say the Messiah is, speaks of him as one superior to himself; as Lord, and as his Lord." Bede (on v. 35) frames the challenge: "if he be a mere man, and only the son of David, how [does] David in the Holy Spirit [call] him Lord?" The Jews "are not reproved for calling him David's son, but for not believing him to be the Son of God." Only the divine sonship of the Messiah resolves the riddle: he is David's son by the flesh, David's Lord by his deity.

The closing note is Mark's own. "The common people heard him gladly," or, as Ellicott renders, "the great body of the people," the stress falling "on the multitude, not on the social condition." Robertson notes the imperfect — "the huge crowd was listening to him gladly" — and that the Sanhedrin's "formal attack that morning to destroy the influence of Jesus with the crowds" had been "a colossal failure": "the crowds were drawn closer to him than before." Barnes and Clarke add that the gospel's "chief success" is "among the common or poorer class," who hear with humbler hearts.

Henry's tradition tempers the gladness with a caution. The people heard him gladly, for his preaching "was surprising and affecting," yet "we do not find that any were wrought upon to believe in him" — "he was to them as a lovely song of one that could play well on an instrument" (cf. Ezekiel 33:32), and "perhaps some of these cried, Crucify him." The witnesses thus close the temple debate with Christ victorious and beloved of the crowd, yet leave a sober warning: to hear the truth gladly is not the same as to believe and obey it. Gloss notes the argument is a clinching of the doubtful question: from David's words it is proved Christ is David's Lord, from the scribes' saying that he is David's son, and the two stand together only in him who is both God and man — the very confession the people's teachers had failed to make.
38:
"And he said unto them in his doctrine, Beware of the scribes, which love to go in long clothing, and love salutations in the marketplaces," The witnesses dwell on the warning and the first marks of the scribes' pride.

The charge is a warning to the people. Robertson notes "Jesus now turns to the multitudes and to his disciples and warns them against the scribes... while they are still there to hear" (cf.
