Matthew 23:1); Mark "gives a mere summary sketch of this bold and terrific indictment" preserved at length in Matthew 23. The idiom "beware of" (blepete apo), Robertson adds, is "a vivid vernacular idiom" — "Beware of the Jews" appears in the papyri.

The first mark is love of display. "To walk in long clothing," Bede explains, "is to go forth into public clad in garments too much ornamented," the sin of "that rich man who fared sumptuously every day." Henry's tradition is careful: "their going in such clothing was not sinful, but their loving to go in it, priding themselves in it, valuing themselves on it" — saying, in effect, with Saul to Samuel, "Honour me now before this people." The second mark is love of public deference: "salutations in the marketplaces," Robertson notes, "where the people could see their dignity recognized," which Theophylact says "they desired... because they wished to be highly esteemed."

The fathers stress that it is the intention, not the office, that Christ condemns. Bede: "He does not forbid that those to whom it falls by the rule of their office should be saluted... or have chief seats"; "He blames the intention and not the office." We are warned of such men "in two ways," Bede adds: "first, we should not be seduced by their hypocrisy into thinking that what they do is good; nor... should we be excited to imitate them." Wesley notes the warning was needful and pointed, yet "no precedent for us: we are not invested with the same authority." The witnesses thus see Christ unmasking, for the people's protection, a religion of robes and recognition — outward dignity coveted for the gratifying of pride.
39:
"And the chief seats in the synagogues, and the uppermost rooms at feasts:" The witnesses dwell on the scribes' craving for precedence in worship and at table.

The catalogue of their vanity continues. The "chief seats in the synagogues" (prōtokathedrias), Robertson explains, were "as a mark of special piety, seats up in front" — and he notes the irony "while now the hypocrites present in church prefer the rear seats." Gill notes these were the places "where the rulers of the synagogue, and the elders of the people, sat" (cf. Luke 11:43). To covet them was to claim, before the assembly, a rank of superior holiness.

The "uppermost rooms at feasts" (prōtoklisias) carry the same craving into social life — the places of honour at the table, "recognizing proper rank and station" (Robertson). Henry's tradition gathers the motive: "they coveted applause, and were fond of it... these pleased a vain fancy; to have these given them, they thought, expressed the value they had for them." The whole bent of the scribes was to be seen, deferred to, and seated above their fellows.

The witnesses note how subtle and persistent this temptation is. Robertson observes that "even the disciples fall victims to this desire for precedence at table" (cf. Luke 22:24, their strife over who should be greatest). Bede had insisted (on v. 38) that Christ "blames the intention and not the office": the seats themselves were lawful, and might rightly fall to men of station; the sin lay in loving and seeking them for the feeding of pride. The witnesses thus see in this love of the best seat — in the house of God and at the feasts of men — a religion turned inside out, where the place that should mark humble service is grasped as a trophy of self-importance. It is a warning, they observe, that searches not the scribes only but every heart that prizes recognition above the lowliness of Christ. Theophylact notes the scribes "desired other things which lead to glory", the whole train of honours hanging together; and that Christ, over against them, "becomes their example in the duties of life", teaching his own to seek the lowest place where the scribes sought the highest.
40:
"Which devour widows' houses, and for a pretence make long prayers: these shall receive greater damnation." The witnesses dwell on the scribes' rapacity, its religious mask, and its heavy doom.

Beneath the show of piety lay greed. "Which devour widows' houses," Barnes explains, means they "devoured the families of widows, or the means of supporting their families," taking advantage "of their ignorance and unprotected state" under "pretence of counselling them." Robertson paints it sharply: "terrible pictures of civil wrong by graft grabbing the homes of helpless widows," whom "they inveigled... into giving their homes." Bede notes such men "hesitate not to receive money from persons who are troubled in conscience, as though they would be their advocates in the judgment"; Theophylact, that they posed as "protectors" of widows "left without... their husbands," and so "devour the houses." Ellicott and Robertson note the construction begins a new sentence — "they that devour... these shall receive."

The "long prayers" were the cloak. The word for "pretence" (prophasei), Robertson notes, means "pretext, pretence of extra piety while robbing the widows." Bede draws the bitter contrast: "a hand stretched out to the poor is always an accompaniment to prayer, but these men pass the night in prayer that they may take away money from the poor."

The doom is the heaviest. "These shall receive greater" — "more abundant" — "condemnation" (Robertson), "greater," says Theophylact, "than the other Jews who sinned." Henry's tradition draws the principle: "as iniquity, thus disguised with a show of piety, is double iniquity, so its doom will be doubly heavy"; "the damnation of hypocrites will be of all others the greatest damnation." He is careful to add that the abuse must not discredit prayer itself: "let not prayers, no nor long prayers, be thought the worse of, if made in humility and sincerity." The witnesses thus close the warning with its sternest stroke: of all sins, the using of religion as a tool to rob the defenceless draws down the severest judgment of God, the Friend of the widow and the fatherless (cf. 2 Timothy 3:6). Robertson notes the comparative "greater" allows, with grim irony, that there are degrees in condemnation — "some comfort in that, at any rate" — but for these robbers in the robe of religion the measure is the fullest.
41:
"And Jesus sat over against the treasury, and beheld how the people cast money into the treasury: and many that were rich cast in much." The witnesses dwell on the scene, the place, and the watching eye of Christ.

Having warned against the vainglory of the scribes, Christ now sits to observe true and false giving. Bede notes "the Lord, who had warned them to avoid the desire of high place and vain glory, now distinguishes by a sure test those who brought in gifts." Gill describes the place: "thirteen chests in the temple" for voluntary offerings and the temple's needs, into which the money was cast; Bede explains the word "treasury" (gazophylacium) means "a place where riches are kept" (cf. John 8:20; 2 Kings 12:9). Theophylact notes "a praiseworthy custom amongst the Jews, that those who were able and willing should put something into the treasury, for the maintenance of the priests, the poor, and the widows."

The rich gave largely, and openly. "Many that were rich cast in much" — and Lightfoot notes how freely "any one might cast in how little soever he would." But Christ's eye was not on the sum but on the heart.

The fathers read the scene allegorically. Bede: "the rich men who cast gifts into the treasury point out the Jews puffed up with the righteousness of the law; the poor widow is the simplicity of the Church" — "poor, because she has cast away the spirit of pride," "a widow, because Jesus her husband has suffered death for her." "The Jew sends of his abundance, because he presumes on his own righteousness; but the Church sends her whole living into God's treasury, because she understands that even her very living is... of Divine grace." The witnesses thus set the stage for the lesson: Christ, the searcher of hearts, sits watching where men think only of the visible gift, ready to weigh not the amount but the love from which it comes. Henry's tradition notes the passage is in Mark and Luke but not in Matthew, recording how Christ, his public teaching done, "sat down, being weary", and yet still observed the givers — for nothing done before him, however small, escapes the notice of him who judges the heart.
42:
"And there came a certain poor widow, and she threw in two mites, which make a farthing." The witnesses dwell on the giver, the smallness of her gift, and the pointed contrast.

She was the poorest of the poor. Robertson notes the word (ptōchē) is "the pauper rather than the mere peasant, the extreme opposite of the rich"; Gill adds she "had no husband to provide for her, and was a poor one, had no substance left her by her husband." Among the throng of wealthy givers, she came alone with almost nothing.

Her gift was the least coin in use. "Two mites" — the word (lepton), Robertson explains, "means peeled or stripped and so very thin," and the two together "were about two-fifths of a cent"; Barnes reckons "about... one third of a cent." The Evangelist explains them by the Roman "farthing" (kodrantes, the quadrans), one of the Latin words, Ellicott notes, "which characterise this Gospel." Lightfoot details the rabbinic reckoning of the coin, observing that "for two mites" this widow "purchased herself an eternal fame, our Saviour himself setting a value upon the thing above all the gifts of them that offered."

The witnesses fasten on the contrast the scene draws. Ellicott observes that "among the many who cast in much must have been some at least of the Pharisees who devoured widows' houses. Here was a widow whose house had been devoured, and who yet showed by her act that she kept the two great commandments" — the very commandments "the scribes themselves declared to be above all burnt offerings." The juxtaposition with the warning just spoken (vv. 38–40) is deliberate: the robbers of widows give grandly of their plunder, while a robbed widow gives all she has. Her two mites, the smallest offering in the temple that day, the witnesses note, were about to be declared by the Lord the greatest. Theophylact reads the widow's gift mystically as "the soul of man", which casts into the temple "two mites, that is, the flesh and the mind" — the flesh by abstinence, the mind by humility — so consecrating all its living and leaving nothing for the world; the smallest gift, rightly understood, is the surrender of the whole self.
43:
"And he called unto him his disciples, and saith unto them, Verily I say unto you, That this poor widow hath cast more in, than all they which have cast into the treasury:" The witnesses dwell on the solemn summons and the new standard of measure.

The calling of the disciples was deliberate. Ellicott notes "the act was significant: he sought to teach them to judge of acts by other than a quantitative standard. For him the widow's mites and the ointment that might have been sold for three hundred pence stood on the same level, so far as each was the expression of a generous and self-sacrificing love." Robertson notes the disciples "had slipped away from him" during the denunciation of the scribes, and he now calls them back for this lesson.

The verdict reverses all appearance. "More than all," Robertson explains, "may mean more than all the rich put together." It was not more in amount, Barnes is careful to say, but "showed more love to the sacred cause, more self-denial, and... more sincerity"; "this is the rule by which God will reward us" (cf. 2 Corinthians 8:12).

Calvin draws the doctrine out fully. "Whatever men offer to God ought to be estimated not by its apparent value... but only by the feeling of the heart"; "the holy affection of him who, according to his small means, offers to God the little that he has, is more worthy of esteem than that of him who offers a hundred times more." The lesson cuts both ways: "the poor who appear not to have the power of doing good are encouraged not to hesitate to express their affection cheerfully out of their slender means"; and "those who possess greater abundance are reminded that it is not enough" merely to outgive the poor in amount — "the chief sacrifice which God requires from us is self-denial." Wesley marvels at "what judgement is cast on the most specious outward actions by the Judge of all, and how acceptable to him is the smallest which springs from self-denying love." The witnesses thus find Christ overturning the world's reckoning: in heaven's accounts the widow's two mites outweigh the rich men's heaps, for God measures the gift by the love and the cost, not the coin.
44:
"For all they did cast in of their abundance; but she of her want did cast in all that she had, even all her living." The witnesses dwell on the contrast of abundance and want, and the totality of the widow's gift.

The rich gave what cost them nothing. Gill notes "they abounded in the things of the world, of which they gave only a part; and though they might give much, yet they could easily spare it." Ellicott sharpens the Greek contrast: "they of their superfluity... she of her deficiency" — and recognises "the same standard of judgment" in Paul's praise of the Macedonian churches, "whose deep poverty had abounded unto the riches of their liberality" (2 Corinthians 8:2).

The widow gave everything. "All that she had, even all her living" — her livelihood, Robertson notes ( bios, "not her life," zōē), the whole of what she had to live on (cf. Deuteronomy 24:6). Barnes: "she trusted in God to supply her wants, and devoted her little property entirely to him." Bede (on v. 41) had read the Church here, which "sends her whole living into God's treasury, because she understands that even her very living is not of her own desert, but of Divine grace." Ellicott sees in Christ's knowledge of "all her living" an instance of "his divine insight into the lives and characters of men," like that shown to the woman of Samaria.

From the scene the witnesses draw the abiding lessons. Barnes lists them: that God is pleased with offerings; that "the highest evidence of love... is not the amount given, but the amount compared with our means"; that "God does not despise the humblest offering, if made in sincerity"; that "there are none who may not in this way show their love"; and that the question for each is "not how much he gives, but how much compared with what he has — how much self-denial he practises, and... the motive." Few, he laments, "practise self-denial for the purposes of charity"; "most give of their abundance — that is, what they can spare without feeling it." The widow's two mites thus close the chapter as a standing rebuke and a standing comfort: a rebuke to ostentatious giving that costs nothing, and a comfort to the poorest, that the smallest gift of self-denying love is great in the eyes of God.
